message:
perf(pager): right-size flat front-cache by observed page count

The ShardedPageCache flat-slot front-cache was previously sized off
the buffer-pool capacity — a lazy high-water bound, not a hot-set
measurement. Every connection open on an empty or tiny database
paid to initialize a 16K-slot lock-free flat table even when only
a handful of pages would ever be touched. The fixed 4096-slot floor
(FLAT_SLOTS_MIN_CAPACITY) amplified the cost.

Changes:
- Drop FLAT_SLOTS_MIN_CAPACITY from 4096 (~11 MiB at 4 KiB pages,
  ~2800 pages at 70% load) to 512 (~1.4 MiB, ~350 pages), still
  enough for tiny databases while keeping connection-open allocation
  proportional to observed working set.
- Factor flat-slot capacity rounding into a shared
  round_flat_slot_capacity() helper (max(1) → next_power_of_two →
  clamp to [MIN, TARGET_CAPACITY]) so both pool-sized and
  initial-pages-sized call sites share identical rounding.
- Add flat_slot_capacity_for_pool() (existing behaviour:
  max_buffers * 2) and flat_slot_capacity_for_initial_pages() (new:
  min(initial_pages, max_buffers) * 2, bounded by the same MIN/MAX
  pair, so an observed 20-page database allocates ~64 slots instead
  of 16384).
- Add ShardedPageCache::with_max_buffers_for_initial_pages +
  with_max_buffers_for_initial_pages_and_shards + shared
  with_pool_and_shards_and_flat_capacity constructor taking an
  explicit flat_capacity so callers with a known initial page count
  can opt into the sized path.
- Existing with_pool_and_shards() preserves the pool-sized behaviour
  by computing flat_slot_capacity_for_pool and delegating, so the
  default buffer-pool-capped ceiling still applies where no initial
  page count is known.

Larger databases still scale up to FLAT_SLOTS_TARGET_CAPACITY
(16384). The pool ceiling remains the upper bound on flat slots.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
